section.title: Job Restored
section.sectionRationale: The book closes with a double reversal. First, God rebukes Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar by name for not having spoken of Him what is right, as His servant Job had, and requires them to be reconciled to God only through Job's own intercessory prayer for them. Then, after Job prays for the very friends who wounded him, the LORD restores his fortunes, doubling everything he had, surrounding him again with family, and giving him a long, full life.
section.thesis: Job's vindication comes not because his suffering is finally explained but because he is proven to have spoken rightly about God, and his restoration comes not merely as compensation for loss but as the fruit of forgiving and interceding for the very friends who had accused him falsely.
section.frameNote: Four movements: God's rebuke of the three friends and His command that they seek reconciliation only through Job's prayer; the LORD's restoration of Job's fortunes after Job prays for his friends, with family and former acquaintances gathering to comfort him; the doubling of Job's flocks and the birth of new children, including three uniquely named and honored daughters; and the closing report of Job's long, full life, dying old and satisfied with days.

units.label: God rebukes the three friends
units.note: you have not spoken of Me what is right

verses.text: After the LORD had spoken these words to Job, He said to Eliphaz the Temanite, “My wrath is kindled against you and your two friends. For you have not spoken about Me accurately, as My servant Job has.

So now, take seven bulls and seven rams, go to My servant Job, and sacrifice a burnt offering for yourselves. Then My servant Job will pray for you, for I will accept his prayer and not deal with you according to your folly. For you have not spoken accurately about Me, as My servant Job has.”
So Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite went and did as the LORD had told them; and the LORD accepted Job’s request.

units.label: The LORD restores Job's fortunes
units.note: after Job had prayed for his friends

verses.text: After Job had prayed for his friends, the LORD {{shuv|restored}} his prosperity and doubled his former possessions.

All his brothers and sisters and prior acquaintances came and dined with him in his house. They consoled him and comforted him over all the adversity that the LORD had brought upon him. And each one gave him a piece of silver and a gold ring.

units.label: A new family, a new blessing
units.note: no women as beautiful as Job's daughters

verses.text: So the LORD blessed Job’s latter days more than his first. He owned 14,000 sheep, 6,000 camels, 1,000 yoke of oxen, and 1,000 female donkeys.
And he also had seven sons and three daughters.
He named his first daughter Jemimah, his second Keziah, and his third Keren-happuch.
No women as beautiful as Job’s daughters could be found in all the land, and their father granted them an {{nachalah|inheritance}} among their brothers.

units.label: Old and full of years
units.note: Job died, old and full of years

verses.text: After this, Job lived 140 years and saw his children and their children to the fourth generation.
And so Job died, old and {{saba|full}} of years.

shuv.gloss: To turn or restore a captivity or fortune, a phrase used elsewhere for national restoration, Deuteronomy 30:3, Psalm 126:1. Its use here elevates Job's personal restoration to the scale of a small exodus, God reversing complete ruin into abundant blessing.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted portion. That Job's three daughters receive an inheritance alongside their seven brothers is culturally remarkable in the ancient world, showing Job's restored household extending even greater honor and provision to his daughters than convention required.

saba.gloss: Satisfied, full, having had enough. The book's closing phrase, 'old and full of years,' uses the same word for contented satisfaction found in blessings elsewhere, Genesis 25:8, Genesis 35:29, marking Job's life as complete and blessed at its end, not merely long.

basic.context: The book closes with God speaking again, this time to rebuke Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar directly, telling them they had not spoken accurately about God, as Job had, and that they must offer sacrifices and seek Job's own prayer on their behalf to avoid God's judgment. Once Job prays for these very friends, the LORD restores everything, doubling his flocks, giving him ten new children, and blessing him with a long, full life, ending with Job living to see four generations of descendants.
basic.hermeneutics: This ending must not be read as proof that suffering always ends in doubled material blessing, as though the book has secretly agreed with the friends' formula after all. The restoration comes specifically after Job's act of costly forgiveness, praying for the men who wounded him, and God's vindication centers on Job having 'spoken accurately' about Him, not on any formula of reward for good behavior.
basic.originalLanguages: God shuv, restores or turns back, Job's fortunes using language elsewhere applied to national restoration from exile, elevating this personal story to a picture of full reversal from ruin. Job's daughters receive a nachalah, inheritance, alongside their brothers, remarkable generosity in their ancient context. And Job dies saba, full and satisfied, with years, the same word used of Abraham's peaceful death.
basic.typology: Job's vindication after unjust suffering, and his role as the one whose intercession secures forgiveness for others, foreshadows Christ, the truly righteous sufferer whose intercession secures forgiveness for those who wronged Him, and whose resurrection is the pattern of restoration beyond suffering that Job's story only partially anticipates.
basic.christ: Job, having suffered unjustly, becomes the very one through whom his accusers find forgiveness, they cannot go to God directly but must come to Job first; this anticipates Jesus Christ, who suffered far more unjustly and now serves as our great High Priest and Intercessor, through whom alone sinners, even those who wronged Him, find reconciliation with God, 1 Timothy 2:5, Hebrews 7:25.
basic.god: God is shown here as both the just Judge who rebukes false theology by name, and the gracious Restorer who blesses His faithful servant beyond his former state; God's justice and mercy operate together, first correcting, then abundantly restoring, without ever explaining to Job the reason for his original suffering.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Timothy 2:5 names Christ as the one Mediator through whom sinners approach God. Hebrews 7:25 says Christ always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him. James 5:11 cites the Lord's compassion and mercy as displayed in how Job's story ends. Deuteronomy 30:3 uses the same 'restore the fortunes' language for God's future restoration of Israel.
basic.oneStory: Job's restoration follows his costly intercession for the friends who wronged him, a pattern the whole Bible carries forward to Jesus Christ, the truly innocent sufferer who intercedes for His enemies and secures a restoration, resurrection life, far greater than anything doubled wealth could picture.

context.literary: The book returns here to prose narrative, matching its opening chapters, forming a frame around the long poetic dialogue; the epilogue answers and resolves several threads from the prologue, Job's family, wealth, and standing before God, all restored or exceeded.
context.historical: Granting daughters an inheritance alongside sons, as Job does for Jemimah, Keziah, and Keren-happuch, went beyond typical ancient Near Eastern and even Israelite legal expectation, where daughters usually inherited only in the absence of sons, Numbers 27:8, making this detail a notable mark of Job's generous restoration.
context.canonical: The phrase 'restored his fortunes,' used elsewhere of Israel's national restoration from exile, Psalm 126:1, Jeremiah 30:3, situates Job's personal story within the Bible's larger pattern of God bringing His people through devastating loss into abundant, unexpected restoration.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To resolve the book by vindicating Job's speech over the friends', requiring Job's own intercessory prayer as the means of the friends' forgiveness, and depicting God's abundant restoration of Job's life as a gracious response to faithfulness and forgiveness, not as confirmation of a strict, automatic doctrine of retribution.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The doubling of Job's specific possessions is a description of what happened to him in his particular story, not a prescriptive guarantee that every faithful sufferer will receive double material blessing in this life; the prescriptive elements are God's commendation of honest speech about Him and the pattern of forgiving and interceding for those who have wronged us.

debates.question: Does Job's restoration in this life mean the book ultimately endorses the friends' doctrine that righteousness brings material prosperity?

views.label: Yes, in the end the friends were partly right
views.case: Job does receive doubled wealth and a new, larger family, which some read as the book quietly confirming that faithfulness is eventually rewarded materially, even if the timing was delayed.

views.label: No, the ending shows grace and vindication, not a formula
views.case: The friends are explicitly rebuked, not commended, in 42:7-8, and Job's restoration is tied specifically to his obedience in interceding for his enemies, not to a general principle that suffering always ends in material gain; many faithful sufferers in Scripture, including ultimately Christ, are not materially restored in this life at all, showing the pattern here is a gracious, particular gift to Job rather than a universal rule.

debates.bearing: The second reading better fits the book's overall argument, which spends thirty seven chapters dismantling the very formula the first reading would reinstate; Job's restoration is best read as God's gracious, specific response to Job's particular faithfulness, not a general law of cause and effect.

debates.question: Why must the three friends seek forgiveness through Job's prayer rather than directly from God?

views.label: A humiliating but fitting consequence for their false accusations
views.case: Having wrongly condemned Job as guilty, they must now be humbled by depending on the very man they condemned to intercede for their own guilt before God.

views.label: A picture of costly, God ordained mediation and forgiveness
views.case: Beyond humiliation, this arrangement pictures a profound pattern: reconciliation with God sometimes runs through reconciliation with the one we have wronged, and Job's willingness to pray for them, rather than withhold forgiveness, is itself presented as an act of grace that anticipates the pattern of forgiveness modeled and commanded throughout the New Testament, Matthew 6:14-15.

debates.bearing: Both elements are present: genuine humbling of the three friends, and a genuine picture of costly intercession and forgiveness that the book presents approvingly as part of Job's restoration, not merely as their punishment.

study.originalLanguages: God shuv, restores, Job's fortunes, language elsewhere used of national return from exile, elevating this personal story to a picture of comprehensive reversal. Job's daughters receive a genuine nachalah, inheritance, alongside their brothers, an act of generosity beyond ordinary custom. And Job dies saba, full and satisfied, with years, the same contented fullness used of the patriarchs' peaceful deaths.

typology.type: Job's intercession securing forgiveness for the friends who wronged him, 42:8-10
typology.fulfillment: Christ's ongoing intercession for those who come to God through Him, Hebrews 7:25, 1 Timothy 2:5.

typology.type: Job's restoration to a full, blessed life after unjust suffering, 42:10-17
typology.fulfillment: The resurrection life secured for believers through Christ's own vindication after unjust suffering, Romans 8:17-18.

study.christ: Job, the unjustly suffering, truly righteous man, becomes the very one whose prayer his accusers desperately need; they cannot approach God directly but must come to Job first. This foreshadows Jesus Christ, the perfectly righteous sufferer who intercedes even for those who wronged Him, praying 'Father, forgive them,' Luke 23:34, and who now lives forever to intercede for all who come to God through Him, Hebrews 7:25, the one Mediator between God and man, 1 Timothy 2:5.
study.god: God is shown here as both just, rebuking false theology by name, and gracious, restoring His servant's life abundantly beyond its former state; His justice and mercy are not in tension but move together, first correcting error, then pouring out blessing, all without ever explaining to Job the specific reason his suffering began.

crossReferences.note: Jesus prays for the forgiveness of those crucifying Him, the fullest expression of the intercessory pattern Job models for his friends.
Christ always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him, the ultimate fulfillment of Job's intercessory role.
Uses the same 'restore the fortunes' language for God's national restoration of His people, echoing Job's personal restoration.
Cites the outcome the Lord brought about for Job as proof that the Lord is full of compassion and mercy.

study.oneStory: The book of Job ends not with an explanation of suffering but with vindication, intercession, and abundant restoration: God rebukes false theology, honors Job's honest faith, and blesses him only after Job forgives and prays for those who wronged him. This whole pattern anticipates Jesus Christ, the truly righteous sufferer who intercedes for His enemies and whose resurrection secures a restoration for all who trust Him that Job's doubled flocks could only begin to picture.

sermon.title: Job: Reversal in Suffering
sermon.why: Piper's sermon is preached directly on Job 42:7-17, tracing how God humbles the three friends and requires Job's intercession for them, and how Job's willingness to forgive and pray for his accusers proves the genuineness of his own repentance before God restores his fortunes.

quiz.q: What does God say to Eliphaz in 42:7?

options.text: That His wrath is kindled against Eliphaz and his two friends because they had not spoken about God accurately, as Job had.
options.note: This is God's direct, explicit verdict vindicating Job's speech over the friends' theology.

options.text: That Job had spoken wrongly and needed to apologize to the friends.
options.note: The verdict is the reverse; God rebukes the friends, not Job, for wrong speech.

options.text: That all four men, including Job, had spoken equally wrongly.
options.note: God singles out the three friends specifically; Job is commended, not condemned, in this verse.

options.text: That the friends should offer sacrifices for Job's sins.
options.note: The friends offer sacrifices for their own guilt, and Job prays for them, not the reverse.

quiz.q: What must the three friends do to avoid God dealing with them according to their folly?

options.text: Offer a burnt offering and have Job pray for them, since God will accept Job's prayer on their behalf.
options.note: Verses 8-9 describe exactly this process, and the friends carry it out.

options.text: Simply apologize directly to God with no further action.
options.note: God specifically requires the sacrifice and Job's intercessory prayer, not merely private apology.

options.text: Give all their wealth to Job.
options.note: The text specifies sacrificial animals and Job's prayer, not a transfer of wealth.

options.text: Convince Job that he was wrong all along.
options.note: The friends are required to humble themselves before Job, not the reverse.

quiz.q: When does the LORD restore Job's fortunes, according to 42:10?

options.text: After Job had prayed for his friends.
options.note: The verse explicitly ties the timing of restoration to Job's act of intercession for the very men who had wronged him.

options.text: Immediately after God's first speech to Job in chapter 38.
options.note: The restoration comes only after this later act of Job's prayer for his friends, in chapter 42.

options.text: Before Job's repentance in 42:1-6.
options.note: Job's repentance and confession, and then his intercession for his friends, both precede the restoration.

options.text: As an automatic reward completely unrelated to anything Job did.
options.note: The text specifically connects restoration to the timing of Job's prayer for his friends.

quiz.q: What is notable about how Job treats his three daughters in 42:13-15?

options.text: He grants them an inheritance alongside their seven brothers, an act of generosity beyond typical ancient custom.
options.note: In the ancient world, daughters typically inherited only in the absence of sons, making Job's provision for his daughters notably generous.

options.text: He excludes them entirely from any inheritance.
options.note: Verse 15 explicitly states he granted them an inheritance among their brothers.

options.text: He gives all his wealth only to his daughters.
options.note: The inheritance is shared between sons and daughters, not given exclusively to the daughters.

options.text: He refuses to name any of his new children.
options.note: Job specifically names all three daughters, Jemimah, Keziah, and Keren-happuch, in verse 14.

quiz.q: How does Job's role in interceding for his friends anticipate Christ?

options.text: It foreshadows Christ's ongoing intercession for those who come to God through Him, even praying for those who wronged Him.
options.note: Hebrews 7:25 and Luke 23:34 both show Christ interceding for and forgiving those who had wronged Him, the pattern Job models here.

options.text: It teaches that intercession for others is unnecessary and unhelpful.
options.note: The passage presents Job's intercession as effective and necessary for his friends' reconciliation with God.

options.text: It shows that only priests, never ordinary believers, can intercede for others.
options.note: Job is not a priest in this narrative; his role as intercessor points forward to the priesthood of all believers made possible through Christ.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to anything in the New Testament.
options.note: This is widely recognized as one of the Old Testament's clearest anticipations of Christ's ongoing priestly intercession.

questions: God vindicates Job's honest speech over the friends' confident theology. Where might you need to trust that honest lament before God matters more to Him than having all the right answers?

Job's restoration comes only after he prays for the very friends who wounded him. Is there someone who has hurt you that God may be calling you to forgive and intercede for, even before you feel fully ready?

Job's daughters receive an inheritance beyond what custom required. Where do you see God's restoration in your own life going beyond simply replacing what was lost, into genuinely new and generous blessing?
The book ends without ever explaining to Job the reason his suffering began. How do you make peace with unresolved questions in your own life while still trusting God's goodness?
Job's intercession for his friends anticipates Christ's ongoing prayer for us. How does knowing Jesus intercedes for you even now change how you approach God in your weakest, most guilty moments?

translationNotes.note: 'You have not spoken about Me accurately' is repeated twice, in verses 7 and 8, for emphasis, underscoring that the friends' theological error, not merely their harshness, is the specific charge against them.

'A piece of silver' translates kesitah, a rare and otherwise obscure unit of currency mentioned elsewhere only in Genesis 33:19 and Joshua 24:32, suggesting an archaic or foreign monetary term appropriate to the patriarchal setting of Job's story.

'Saw his children and their children to the fourth generation' is itself a traditional biblical picture of a full, blessed life, paralleling the patriarchal narratives and underscoring that Job's restoration extended across his whole remaining lifetime, not merely a single reversal of fortune.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 42:7-17. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
